one_line_takeaway: The paper shows that a query-independent 'turn-amplifying' activation direction exists in conversational LLMs and can be exploited via activation steering, LoRA fine-tuning (supply-chain), or as few as 25 targeted bit flips (runtime/Rowhammer) to multiply multi-turn interaction cost by up to ~9.9x turns and ~200x input tokens while keeping each individual response benign and largely preserving task accuracy, and that tested defenses fail to meaningfully mitigate it.
key_finding: Activation steering with the learned universal direction is the most effective method tested, increasing turns/input-tokens/output-tokens by 8.6x/88.2x/4.6x on average (worst case 9.9x/200.1x/6.4x) versus 5.0-5.8x/31.9-46.3x/1.3-1.5x for the Prefilling/System Message baselines, translating to an estimated 9.6x average per-conversation cost increase (vs 3.2-3.7x for baselines) under an assumed 5x output-token cost multiplier. The method wins in 42/48 evaluated model/dataset/mode combinations and largely preserves task accuracy (change range -20.2% to +8.1%, comparable to baselines' -12.4% to +3.2%). Mechanistically, early-layer interventions are most effective (layer 0 alone reaches 85.9-94.5% of full multi-layer steering performance vs. 1.5-30.1% at the final layer), and natural (unsteered) conversations show 8-51% higher cosine similarity to the learned direction during turn-prolonged interactions than in default single-turn ones, indicating the direction corresponds to a pre-existing internal representation rather than an artifact of the intervention. Practically, LoRA fine-tuning of only 3.3x10^-2% to 9.6x10^-1% of parameters induces 6.6-9.2x more turns (11.7-116.5x input tokens, 2.0-6.0x output tokens; increasing r from 1 to 16 gives only 1.5-14.4% marginal gain, suggesting a low-rank encoding). Bit-flip attacks modifying only 25 weights (3.1-8.3x10^-7% of parameters) induce 3.4x more turns / 18.6x input tokens / 1.6x output tokens on Alpaca (weaker, 1.3-13.2x, on GSM8K, attributed to poor generalization from Alpaca-derived bit search), with accuracy degradation limited to -11.5% to -5.4%. Tested defenses (a defensive system message; 5-shot direct-answer prompting) are largely ineffective against steering and LoRA attacks: the system message reduces turns only marginally except against bit-flip (37-57% reduction); few-shot prompting is more effective (reducing System-Message-baseline turns by 78.8%, steering by 23.5%, LoRA by 16.7%) but itself imposes prohibitive overhead (58x more input tokens, 2.7x more output tokens even for benign queries).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'turn amplification': a novel cost-amplification failure mode/attack against conversational LLMs in which a model is induced to persistently issue clarification-seeking responses, prolonging multi-turn interactions without ever completing the user's underlying task, while each individual response remains benign and policy-compliant. Unlike prior single-turn cost-amplification work that requires per-prompt manipulation at inference time, the authors show turn amplification arises from conversational dynamics and can be induced by (1) activation steering using a query-independent 'universal' direction discovered via a gradient-based optimization method (not simple difference-in-means, because the behavior is not reliably elicited by default), (2) supply-chain LoRA fine-tuning implanted into a distributed model, and (3) runtime bit-flip corruption of as few as 25 weights (via a Rowhammer-style threat model). They also build a first-of-its-kind interaction-level auditing framework (LLM-as-Judge based) to measure turn-amplifying propensity by repurposing single-turn QA benchmarks (Alpaca, GSM8K) into multi-turn evaluation loops, and they evaluate existing/preliminary defenses, finding them largely ineffective.
threat_model: Adversary does not need control over user inputs at inference time (distinguishing this from prior prompt-level cost-amplification attacks). Three concrete attack vectors/scenarios are instantiated: (A1) a malicious model integrator who modifies internal activations at runtime via activation steering; (A2) a supply-chain adversary who LoRA fine-tunes and distributes a backdoored model via public hubs (e.g., HuggingFace) with white-box access to the target model, consistent with prior model-backdooring threat models; (A3) an infrastructure/co-tenant adversary in a resource-sharing (multi-tenant cloud) environment who induces targeted GPU bit flips via Rowhammer (citing GPUHammer and PrisonBreak-style prior work) to corrupt ~25 weights persistently until the model is reloaded. In all cases the goal is to inflate operational/inference cost (turns, input tokens, output tokens) by biasing conversational dynamics toward clarification-seeking, not to compromise output safety/quality directly.
limitations: Ground truth for 'task completion' relies entirely on an LLM-as-Judge (Qwen2.5-32B) rather than real users, which the authors acknowledge is a practicality-driven substitute for genuine human-in-the-loop studies. The bit-flip attack's weaker/less consistent transfer to GSM8K (attributed to the bit-search being conducted on general Alpaca-style data) shows limited cross-domain generalization for that specific attack vector. Accuracy can degrade meaningfully under attack in the worst case (up to -20.2% for Qwen2.5-3B in Hard mode), and the authors note smaller models appear more sensitive to activation-level interventions. All demonstrations are restricted to open-source models (3B-22B) in controlled research settings; the Impact Statement explicitly states the work does not provide turnkey exploitation tools for deployed production systems. No fully effective defense is identified; the paper's own evaluated countermeasures are described as 'largely ineffective' or impractically costly, and the paper explicitly calls for future defense work rather than providing one.

datasets_used: Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023) - 5,000 prompts for synthetic training dialogues, 250 held-out prompts for evaluation
GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) - 250 prompts for evaluation, used to also compute answer accuracy
quantitative_results: Activation steering: average 8.6x turns, 88.2x input tokens, 4.6x output tokens increase across models/datasets/modes; worst case 9.9x/200.1x/6.4x
Steering wins in 42 of 48 evaluated model-dataset-mode combinations
Steering accuracy change range: -20.2% to +8.1% (largest drop: Qwen2.5-3B, Hard mode, -20.2%)
Baselines (Prefilling/System Message) average: 5.0-5.8x turns, 31.9-46.3x input tokens, 1.3-1.5x output tokens; -12.4% to +3.2% accuracy change
Estimated per-conversation cost increase (5x output-token cost weighting): 9.6x for steering vs 3.2-3.7x for baselines
Layer localization (Qwen2.5-3B): layer-0-only steering reaches 85.9-94.5% of full multi-layer steering effectiveness; intermediate layers (5-20) reach 58.9-90.2%; final layer drops to 1.5-30.1%
Cosine similarity of learned direction to natural activations increases 8-51% in turn-prolonged vs default conversations (Llama3-8B, layer 10)

LoRA fine-tuning (Qwen2.5-3B, Llama3-8B; r=1 and r=16): trains 3.3x10^-2% to 9.6x10^-1% of parameters; induces 6.6-9.2x turns, 11.7-116.5x input tokens, 2.0-6.0x output tokens; r=16 over r=1 gives only 1.5-14.4% marginal gain; 3.8-15.2x estimated usage cost increase
Bit-flip attack: modifies 25 weights (3.1-8.3x10^-7% of parameters); Alpaca: 3.4x turns, 18.6x input tokens, 1.6x output tokens; GSM8K: weaker, 1.3-13.2x; accuracy change -11.5% to -5.4%

Defenses on Llama3-8B/Alpaca: defensive system message reduces bit-flip attack turns by 37-57% but has minimal effect elsewhere; few-shot prompting reduces System-Message-baseline turns by 78.8%, steering by 23.5%, LoRA by 16.7%, but increases input tokens 58x and output tokens 2.7x even for benign No-Attack queries
baselines_compared: Prefilling (assistant response prefixed with 'Can you clarify')
System Message (instructs model to avoid answering and prolong interaction)
